name: Drop Bear
description: The Drop Bear is a mythical Australian marsupial, a larger, more ferocious, and predatory version of the koala. Unlike its herbivorous cousin, the Drop Bear is a carnivore that ambushes its prey from above. It lurks in the treetops, patiently waiting for unsuspecting victims to pass underneath. Once a target is within range, the Drop Bear will drop from the branches, using its sharp claws and teeth to inflict serious injury. Folklore suggests that Drop Bears specifically target tourists and those who haven't taken precautions such as applying Vegemite behind their ears or speaking loudly in Australian slang. The Drop Bear is a staple of Australian tall tales, often used to playfully scare visitors and add a touch of danger to the otherwise idyllic Australian bush. While no concrete evidence of their existence has ever been found, the legend of the Drop Bear persists, fueled by campfire stories and the occasional prank.
subtitle: Arboreal predator of the Australian bush
appearance: The Drop Bear is depicted as a large, muscular koala with reddish-orange fur. It has sharp claws and teeth, much larger than those of a regular koala. Its eyes are often described as being red or yellow, giving it a menacing appearance. Some accounts also mention a more flattened face and a more aggressive posture than a typical koala.
habitat: Eucalyptus forests throughout Australia, particularly in areas with dense foliage and tall trees.
behavior: Drop Bears are solitary creatures, spending most of their time in the treetops, waiting for prey. They are ambush predators, relying on stealth and surprise to catch their victims. They are said to be more active at night. They are extremely territorial and aggressive, especially towards perceived threats.

keyMyths.title: The Tourist's Terror
keyMyths.description: Tourists are particularly vulnerable to Drop Bear attacks, often unaware of the danger lurking above. Applying Vegemite behind the ears is said to be a deterrent, though the effectiveness is debated. The myth serves as a humorous warning to visitors.

sources.author: Bill Bryson
sources.description: Brief mention of the Drop Bear as a common Australian joke.
sources.text: Brief mention of the Drop Bear as a common Australian joke.
